data.title: Instalco (OM:INSTAL) Thin 2.5% Net Margin Keeps Bullish Growth Narratives Under Scrutiny
data.description:
Instalco (OM:INSTAL) has opened Q1 2026 earnings season with a set of numbers that put recent revenue scale and EPS front and centre, with Q4 2025 revenue at SEK3,766 million and basic EPS at SEK0.37 helping to frame the latest results against a 12 month net profit margin of 2.5%. Over the past few quarters, the company has seen revenue fluctuate between SEK3,027 million and SEK3,766 million while quarterly EPS has ranged from SEK0.19 to SEK0.45, giving investors a concrete view of how SEK13.6 billion in trailing 12 month revenue translated into SEK1.28 of EPS over the same period. With earnings forecast to grow faster than revenue and margins still relatively thin, this update puts profit conversion and any signs of margin traction firmly in focus for investors.

See our full analysis for Instalco.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up against the prevailing stories about Instalco's growth potential, risk profile, and profit quality that investors have been following over the past year.

See what the community is saying about Instalco

Advertisement

2.5% net margin keeps profit quality in focus

  • Over the last 12 months, Instalco produced SEK13.6b in revenue and SEK344m in net income, which works out to a 2.5% net margin that leaves only a small slice of each krona of sales as profit.
  • Supporters with a bullish view point to higher margin areas like technical consulting and automation as potential profit drivers, yet the current 2.5% net margin and trailing five year earnings decline of about 10.4% per year show that any margin improvement still needs to come through in the reported numbers.
    • Analysts expecting roughly 20.2% yearly earnings growth and around 7% yearly revenue growth are effectively arguing that higher margin work and efficiency efforts can lift returns on that SEK13.6b revenue base.
    • At the same time, the current margin and the SEK344m of trailing net income give you a concrete check against those bullish expectations, because they show where profitability sits today before any future improvement.

Strong believers in the upside case often build their story around higher margin segments and earnings growth potential, so if you want to see how that thinking is built out in full, 🐂 Instalco Bull Case

Forecast 20.2% earnings growth vs modest history

  • Earnings are forecast to grow about 20.2% per year while revenue is expected to rise roughly 7% per year, which contrasts with the trailing five year earnings decline of about 10.4% per year and highlights how different the outlook is from the recent track record.
  • Investors who lean on a more cautious, bearish view often question whether this step up from a 10.4% yearly earnings decline to a 20.2% yearly growth forecast is realistic, especially with only a 2.5% net margin as a starting point.
    • Bears highlight that weak organic revenue in core construction markets and a modest margin base could limit how much of that 7% forecast revenue growth turns into bottom line progress.
    • They also point to reliance on acquisitions and higher debt as factors that could make it harder for actual earnings to track the 20.2% yearly growth that the forecasts are implying.

If you want to see how skeptics connect these growth forecasts to risks around margins, debt, and acquisitions in more detail, 🐻 Instalco Bear Case

P/E and DCF send mixed price signals

  • At a share price of SEK37.88, Instalco trades on a 29.6x P/E, which is lower than the 47.5x peer average but higher than the 15.3x European Construction industry P/E, and also sits well below the DCF fair value of SEK93.62 that has been calculated for the shares.
  • What stands out for valuation focused investors is the tension between a DCF fair value almost 2.5x the current price and the mixed P/E picture, where Instalco looks cheaper than closer peers but more expensive than the wider construction group.
    • The roughly 59.5% gap between the SEK37.88 share price and the SEK93.62 DCF fair value is often used by optimistic investors to argue that the market is not fully reflecting the forecast 20.2% yearly earnings growth.
    • On the other hand, the higher P/E relative to the broader industry and the 2.5% net margin give cautious investors a reason to question whether the current profitability profile supports that DCF gap without the forecast improvements actually being delivered.

This is not good as insiders invest based on expectations that their money will appreciate over time. However, as a result of recent losses, their original investment is now worth only UK£196.9k.

While insider transactions are not the most important thing when it comes to long-term investing, we would consider it foolish to ignore insider transactions altogether.

data.title: Vitec Software Group's (STO:VIT B) Profits May Not Reveal Underlying Issues
data.description:
Vitec Software Group AB (publ)'s (STO:VIT B) robust recent earnings didn't do much to move the stock. We think this is due to investors looking beyond the statutory profits and being concerned with what they see.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

In order to understand the potential for per share returns, it is essential to consider how much a company is diluting shareholders. As it happens, Vitec Software Group issued 6.2% more new shares over the last year. That means its earnings are split among a greater number of shares. To celebrate net income while ignoring dilution is like rejoicing because you have a single slice of a larger pizza, but ignoring the fact that the pizza is now cut into many more slices. Check out Vitec Software Group's historical EPS growth by clicking on this link.

Advertisement

How Is Dilution Impacting Vitec Software Group's Earnings Per Share (EPS)?

Vitec Software Group has improved its profit over the last three years, with an annualized gain of 73% in that time. But EPS was only up 57% per year, in the exact same period. And in the last year the company managed to bump profit up by 11%. But in comparison, EPS only increased by 8.0% over the same period. And so, you can see quite clearly that dilution is influencing shareholder earnings.

Changes in the share price do tend to reflect changes in earnings per share, in the long run. So it will certainly be a positive for shareholders if Vitec Software Group can grow EPS persistently. But on the other hand, we'd be far less excited to learn profit (but not EPS) was improving. For the ordinary retail shareholder, EPS is a great measure to check your hypothetical "share" of the company's profit.

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

Our Take On Vitec Software Group's Profit Performance

Each Vitec Software Group share now gets a meaningfully smaller slice of its overall profit, due to dilution of existing shareholders. Because of this, we think that it may be that Vitec Software Group's statutory profits are better than its underlying earnings power. But at least holders can take some solace from the 57% per annum growth in EPS for the last three. Of course, we've only just scratched the surface when it comes to analysing its earnings; one could also consider margins, forecast growth, and return on investment, among other factors. Keep in mind, when it comes to analysing a stock it's worth noting the risks involved. At Simply Wall St, we found 1 warning sign for Vitec Software Group and we think they deserve your attention.

data.title: Saudia Dairy & Foodstuff Company (TADAWUL:2270) Just Reported, And Analysts Assigned A ر.س273 Price Target
data.description:
Last week, you might have seen that Saudia Dairy & Foodstuff Company (TADAWUL:2270) released its quarterly result to the market. The early response was not positive, with shares down 2.7% to ر.س208 in the past week. Revenues came in 4.9% below expectations, at ر.س738m. Statutory earnings per share were relatively better off, with a per-share profit of ر.س14.92 being roughly in line with analyst estimates. This is an important time for investors, as they can track a company's performance in its report, look at what experts are forecasting for next year, and see if there has been any change to expectations for the business. With this in mind, we've gathered the latest statutory forecasts to see what the analysts are expecting for next year.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Taking into account the latest results, the most recent consensus for Saudia Dairy & Foodstuff from four analysts is for revenues of ر.س3.26b in 2026. If met, it would imply a decent 10% increase on its revenue over the past 12 months. Statutory earnings per share are forecast to shrink 9.6% to ر.س12.55 in the same period. Before this earnings report, the analysts had been forecasting revenues of ر.س3.23b and earnings per share (EPS) of ر.س11.42 in 2026. The analysts seems to have become more bullish on the business, judging by their new earnings per share estimates.

Check out our latest analysis for Saudia Dairy & Foodstuff

The consensus price target fell 5.8% to ر.س273, suggesting the increase in earnings forecasts was not enough to offset other the analysts concerns. That's not the only conclusion we can draw from this data however, as some investors also like to consider the spread in estimates when evaluating analyst price targets. Currently, the most bullish analyst values Saudia Dairy & Foodstuff at ر.س317 per share, while the most bearish prices it at ر.س215. These price targets show that analysts do have some differing views on the business, but the estimates do not vary enough to suggest to us that some are betting on wild success or utter failure.

Another way we can view these estimates is in the context of the bigger picture, such as how the forecasts stack up against past performance, and whether forecasts are more or less bullish relative to other companies in the industry. The analysts are definitely expecting Saudia Dairy & Foodstuff's growth to accelerate, with the forecast 14% annualised growth to the end of 2026 ranking favourably alongside historical growth of 8.7% per annum over the past five years. By contrast, our data suggests that other companies (with analyst coverage) in a similar industry are forecast to grow their revenue at 5.7% per year. Factoring in the forecast acceleration in revenue, it's pretty clear that Saudia Dairy & Foodstuff is expected to grow much faster than its industry.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The biggest takeaway for us is the consensus earnings per share upgrade, which suggests a clear improvement in sentiment around Saudia Dairy & Foodstuff's earnings potential next year. Happily, there were no major changes to revenue forecasts, with the business still expected to grow faster than the wider industry. The consensus price target fell measurably, with the analysts seemingly not reassured by the latest results, leading to a lower estimate of Saudia Dairy & Foodstuff's future valuation.

Following on from that line of thought, we think that the long-term prospects of the business are much more relevant than next year's earnings. We have forecasts for Saudia Dairy & Foodstuff going out to 2028, and you can see them free on our platform here.

data.title: Sparc AI builds momentum in Ukraine with yet another distribution channel
data.description:
Advertisement

Key Points

  • Sparc AI (CSE:SPAI) continues to build momentum in Ukraine, forming yet another partnership with a member of the Ukrainian National Guard.
  • This news comes just a few days after it signed its second Ukrainian drone manufacturer as a partner. This adds to Sparc AI lists of partnerships, which include another manufacturer in India, a distribution agreement with Precision Technic Defense Group and a group working closely with the UAE Ministry of Defence.
  • This partnership adds another layer to Sparc AI’s growth, as outlined in this community narrative that explains how Sparc AI is tacking the growing drone industry.

Sparc AI (CSE: SPAI) has now signed an agreement with a member of the Ukrainian National Guard involved in drone pilot training.

The deal adds another distribution channel for its Overwatch platform, expanding its reach beyond the existing manufacturer partnerships.

Importantly, this update follows closely on the company’s recent announcement of a second Ukrainian drone manufacturer partnership, suggesting a rapid build-out of its presence in the region.

Management’s approach is to build multiple routes to market, combining OEM integrations with direct access to operators. To support this, Sparc AI has also moved its local agent into a full-time role to strengthen execution in Ukraine.

Building a network in a live combat environment

Ukraine is emerging as a central node in Sparc AI’s strategy. It offers something few markets can: constant exposure to real-world GPS disruption.

Overwatch uses software to correct navigation drift by analysing data from existing sensors, removing the need for costly hardware upgrades.

Each new integration, whether through manufacturers or operators, feeds more data into the system. That creates a flywheel where usage improves performance, and better performance supports further adoption.

By pairing recent manufacturer deals with operator-level access, Sparc AI appears to be building both sides of that loop at once.

What this means for scaling Overwatch

Taken together, the recent updates point to a shift from isolated partnerships to a broader distribution strategy.

Sparc AI is layering its approach:

  • manufacturers to embed the software
  • operators to validate and use it in the field

If successful, this dual-channel model could accelerate adoption.

For now though this is still early-stage traction. None of these agreements guarantee revenue.

The investment case still depends on two things:

  1. consistent real-world performance
  2. conversion of partnerships into scaled deployments

Until those are proven, execution risk remains high.

A market shaped by GPS disruption

The backdrop is a growing global issue. GPS jamming and spoofing are becoming more common particularly in conflict zones.

At the same time, drone usage is expanding rapidly across both defence and commercial sectors.

This is driving demand for alternative navigation solutions. Sparc AI enters the market with a software-based approach that’s a scalable, lower-cost option compared to hardware changes.

If Overwatch proves effective in high-intensity environments like Ukraine, it could position Sparc AI within a fast-growing segment of the autonomy stack.

What investors think about Sparc AI

This narrative on Sparc AI written by an investor positions Sparc AI as an early-stage company tackling a large and growing problem.

It estimates a fair value of around $5.25 per share, based on a very conservative scenario where the company scales to approximately 10,000 deployed devices generating recurring licensing revenue.

As the author notes:

“Even a small share of deployed devices… is enough to support meaningful revenue and justify a higher valuation.”

This latest update, especially when viewed alongside the recent Ukrainian manufacturer partnerships, strengthens the distribution and adoption side of that equation.

However, the most important assumption remains unproven: whether Overwatch consistently performs in real-world conditions and converts early access into repeat deployments.

See the full narrative.

About Sparc AI

Sparc AI is a defence technology company focused on addressing a key challenge in autonomous systems: maintaining accurate navigation when GPS is unavailable.

Its flagship product, Overwatch, is an AI-driven software platform that enhances positioning by using data from sensors already embedded in drones and other systems. This approach avoids the need for costly hardware upgrades while supporting scalable deployment across large fleets.

The company remains in the early stages of commercialisation, with a strategy centred on integrating its software into drone manufacturers’ systems and expanding across defence, and potentially broader commercial, applications.

data.title: Why It Might Not Make Sense To Buy Sanofi (EPA:SAN) For Its Upcoming Dividend
data.description:
Regular readers will know that we love our dividends at Simply Wall St, which is why it's exciting to see Sanofi (EPA:SAN) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next four days. The ex-dividend date is two business days before a company's record date in most cases, which is the date on which the company determines which shareholders are entitled to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is important because any transaction on a stock needs to have been settled before the record date in order to be eligible for a dividend. Thus, you can purchase Sanofi's shares before the 5th of May in order to receive the dividend, which the company will pay on the 7th of May.

The company's upcoming dividend is €4.12 a share, following on from the last 12 months, when the company distributed a total of €4.12 per share to shareholders. Last year's total dividend payments show that Sanofi has a trailing yield of 5.3% on the current share price of €78.32. Dividends are an important source of income to many shareholders, but the health of the business is crucial to maintaining those dividends. So we need to investigate whether Sanofi can afford its dividend, and if the dividend could grow.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Dividends are typically paid from company earnings. If a company pays more in dividends than it earned in profit, then the dividend could be unsustainable. Sanofi paid out 102% of its earnings, which is more than we're comfortable with, unless there are mitigating circumstances. A useful secondary check can be to evaluate whether Sanofi generated enough free cash flow to afford its dividend. Dividends consumed 70% of the company's free cash flow last year, which is within a normal range for most dividend-paying organisations.

It's disappointing to see that the dividend was not covered by profits, but cash is more important from a dividend sustainability perspective, and Sanofi fortunately did generate enough cash to fund its dividend. Still, if the company repeatedly paid a dividend greater than its profits, we'd be concerned. Extraordinarily few companies are capable of persistently paying a dividend that is greater than their profits.

Check out our latest analysis for Sanofi

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Businesses with shrinking earnings are tricky from a dividend perspective. If earnings decline and the company is forced to cut its dividend, investors could watch the value of their investment go up in smoke. Sanofi's earnings per share have fallen at approximately 16% a year over the previous five years. Ultimately, when earnings per share decline, the size of the pie from which dividends can be paid, shrinks.

Many investors will assess a company's dividend performance by evaluating how much the dividend payments have changed over time. In the last 10 years, Sanofi has lifted its dividend by approximately 3.8% a year on average. That's intriguing, but the combination of growing dividends despite declining earnings can typically only be achieved by paying out a larger percentage of profits. Sanofi is already paying out 102% of its profits, and with shrinking earnings we think it's unlikely that this dividend will grow quickly in the future.

To Sum It Up

Is Sanofi an attractive dividend stock, or better left on the shelf? It's never fun to see a company's earnings per share in retreat. Worse, Sanofi's paying out a majority of its earnings and more than half its free cash flow. Positive cash flows are good news but it's not a good combination. With the way things are shaping up from a dividend perspective, we'd be inclined to steer clear of Sanofi.

Although, if you're still interested in Sanofi and want to know more, you'll find it very useful to know what risks this stock faces. In terms of investment risks, we've identified 2 warning signs with Sanofi and understanding them should be part of your investment process.

If you're in the market for strong dividend payers, we recommend checking our selection of top dividend stocks.

data.title: Bharat Coking Coal's (NSE:BHARATCOAL) Anemic Earnings Might Be Worse Than You Think
data.description:
The market wasn't impressed with the soft earnings from Bharat Coking Coal Limited (NSE:BHARATCOAL) recently. We did some further digging and think they have a few more reasons to be concerned beyond the statutory profit.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Examining Cashflow Against Bharat Coking Coal's Earnings

One key financial ratio used to measure how well a company converts its profit to free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio. To get the accrual ratio we first subtract FCF from profit for a period, and then divide that number by the average operating assets for the period. This ratio tells us how much of a company's profit is not backed by free cashflow.

As a result, a negative accrual ratio is a positive for the company, and a positive accrual ratio is a negative. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. That's because some academic studies have suggested that high accruals ratios tend to lead to lower profit or less profit growth.

Bharat Coking Coal has an accrual ratio of 0.23 for the year to March 2026. We can therefore deduce that its free cash flow fell well short of covering its statutory profit. Over the last year it actually had negative free cash flow of ₹13b, in contrast to the aforementioned profit of ₹1.28b. Coming off the back of negative free cash flow last year, we imagine some shareholders might wonder if its cash burn of ₹13b, this year, indicates high risk.

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of Bharat Coking Coal.

Our Take On Bharat Coking Coal's Profit Performance

Bharat Coking Coal's accrual ratio for the last twelve months signifies cash conversion is less than ideal, which is a negative when it comes to our view of its earnings. Because of this, we think that it may be that Bharat Coking Coal's statutory profits are better than its underlying earnings power. Sadly, its EPS was down over the last twelve months.
